nodes.title: Detailer LoRAs
widgets_values:
Blocking Other LoRAs

It is possible to block other LoRAs by connecting this base model to the Detailer LoRA Loader. You may wish to do this if you find your LoRAs are turning hands into macaroni alfredo or something. If you find that your LoRA set is interfering in some way, connect **model** directly from the checkpoint loader node directly to this LoRA loader. Delete the connection when you wish to return to normal mode. To facilitate this, a base model node which mirrors the selected model is located here.

nodes.title: SEGS Detailer Array
widgets_values:
The SEGS Detailer lets you choose how to fill in critical details using BBOX detectors.

The VRAM has to be cleaned between Detailer passes to prevent bleed-over artifacts, particularly if the bbox is large. BBox detection goes from left to right by default and top to bottom. Note that there will be outputs showing the bbox preview but if a bbox is not found, a preview from the previous successful detection will still remain in the preview box - in other words, it will look like it is detecting, but it is not. One telltale sign of this is if the bboxes are out of order.

Once you are satisfied with the generated results you can fix the seed and work on details.

"Work on Details" means using the prompts and the Primitives to home in on a better result. For instance, adjusting facial expressions and eye color. Feel free to use natural language.

A detailer LoRA is provided but you may need to configure it yourself. It should help you achieve better results than without.

**Remember, detailers use denoise settings** and these can make a big difference.

The detector will not always detect what is there. Lowering the threshold of the BBOX Detector makes detection easier, while raising it can help filter out false positives. Default is 0.5.

For a more detailed guide on how to use these detailer arrays, see the link below:

https://civitai.com/articles/26885/using-the-bbox-sorter-mgherders-detailer-array

nodes.title: Dual Image Saver Information
widgets_values:
Image Saver

This workflow features **3 Image Saver Nodes:** 2 within the Spaghetti Region and 1 within the Upscale Group. Edit these nodes to change: * File Name * Directory * Metadata Options * Embed Workflow options **One practical way to run this workflow is to disable the first pass image saver, but if you find you prefer to have both copies of the output, you can enable it.** Click the grey dot on the left side of a node to quickly expand/collapse it.

nodes.title: QuickStart Guide
widgets_values:
SDXL TEXT2IMG

This is a t2i workflow. It has a separate metadata-inclusive save node for the First Pass, after the Detailer, and after Upscale.

Order of Operations

0) Configure Generation Settings 1) Image Generation with Preview 2) Hires Fix (increase size of image by percentage, use above node to define it) 3) Save First Pass 4) Upscale & Save 5) Hand Detailer 6) Face Detailer 7) Final Save If both Upscale and Hires Fix is skipped, detailer results are unlikely to improve smaller bboxes.

For Speed:

Skip Hires and Detailer, Use Upscaler Only

For Quality:

When you really have an image you like, Freeze the seed, and enable HiresFix and Upscaler. If you enable the Detailer, check the prompts and run a pass. Check the SEGS previews and adjust the prompts accordingly. Use the Segm If images suddenly come out high contrast and jumbled, check the CFG Controls toggle and your CFG. If CFG controls are off, your CFG may need to be reduced.

nodes.title: Toggle Information
widgets_values:
# Guidance Control Nodes: Dynamic Thresholding + Skimmed CFG

These two nodes work together to let you use **higher CFG values** (e.g., 7–12) while keeping generations stable, clean, and natural-looking.

## Dynamic Thresholding
**Purpose**: Prevents the model from pushing pixel values too extremely during sampling.  
**What it fixes**:  
- Blown-out highlights / crushed shadows  
- Overly harsh contrast  
- Strange color saturation or intensity spikes  
- General instability / artifacts when CFG is high  

**In practice**: It acts like an "automatic safety cap" on extreme activations, helping the image stay balanced and coherent even when the prompt is being followed very strongly.  
Typical settings: Mimic CFG 7.5–9.0, Top percentile ~0.95–0.99, Thresholding method "percentile" or "constant".

## Skimmed CFG (CFG Rescale / Skimmed Guidance)
**Purpose**: Softens the aggressive side-effects of high CFG without losing prompt strength.  
**What it fixes**:  
- Stiff / plastic-looking poses and anatomy  
- Oversaturation or unnatural color vibrancy  
- That "forced" or over-cooked appearance high-CFG images often get  
- Loss of fine details / mid-tone subtlety  

**In practice**: It scales back the CFG influence gradually (especially in later steps), making guidance feel smoother and less destructive. You still get strong prompt adherence, but with more natural gradients and less "burn-in".  
Common range: Rescale factor 0.4–0.7, Phi 0.8–1.2 (adjust based on model).

## Why use them together?
- **Dynamic Thresholding** → controls raw value extremes (keeps the image from breaking)  
- **Skimmed CFG** → smooths the overall guidance curve (keeps the image from looking artificial)  

Combined, they allow **CFG 8–12** (or even higher on some merges) while avoiding the usual downsides: blown contrast, garish colors, stiffness, or random artifacts.

### Quick Starting Tips
- Base CFG: 7.5–10 (test higher with these nodes active)  
- Dynamic Thresholding: Mimic CFG ~7.5–9, Top percentile 0.97–0.995  
- Skimmed CFG: Rescale 0.5–0.7, Phi ~1.0  
- Disable one at a time to see which effect you prefer for a given model/prompt  
- On anime/realism merges: often need slightly stronger settings than on pure photoreal models

These nodes are especially valuable for SDXL-based workflows and merges that respond aggressively to high guidance (like many anime/realism checkpoints in 2025–2026).

Happy generating — push that CFG and keep the image sane! 🚀

nodes.title: Upscaler 1.1
widgets_values:
# Upscaler Nodes

The Upscaler is triggered before the detailer, and is designed to hopefully increase the overall quality of the image without introducing hallucinations.

I tried to incorporate the Lightning LoRA here but it did not improve generation speed. However, I did find almost the same results in overall generation with just 8 steps instead of 30+ steps.

The upscaler's seed is tied to the main generation.
